# What are WebSockets ws:// & wss:// connections

0 MIN READ • [Developer Relations Team](https://www.pubnub.com/blog/author/developer-relations/) on Sep 3, 2023

What is WebSockets Protocol? Implementation and Use Cases

# What is RFC 6455: The WebSocket Protocol

WebSocket is a communication protocol providing full-duplex, bidirectional communication channels over a single, long-lived [TCP connection](https://www.pubnub.com/guides/tcp-ip/). It enables real-time data exchange between a client (e.g., web browser) and a server.

Connections are established with an HTTP/HTTPS handshake using `ws://` or `wss://` schemes, where the client sends an `Upgrade: websocket` header, and the server responds with `101 Switching Protocols`. This upgrades the connection from HTTP to the WebSocket protocol.

It supports text and binary data formats and employs a lightweight framing mechanism for data transfer. Commonly used in applications like chat, gaming, IoT, and live updates.

## How do WebSockets work?

WebSockets communication process starts with the client sending an HTTP request including a "Connection: Upgrade" header to initiate a WebSocket handshake. In response, the server sends an HTTP 101 status code, confirming the protocol switch to WebSocket. Once the connection is established, it facilitates real-time, bidirectional communication without additional HTTP requests. Messages are transmitted, and the connection remains active until it is explicitly closed by either side or interrupted by network issues.

It is important to note that when running on the WebSocket protocol layer, WebSockets require a uniform resource identifier (URI) to use a `ws:` or `wss:` scheme, similar to how [HTTP](https://www.pubnub.com/guides/http/) URLs will always use an `http:` or `https:` scheme.

Google Chrome was the first browser to include standard support for WebSockets in 2009. [RFC 6455](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6455)—The WebSocket Protocol—was officially published online in 2011. [The Google WebSocket Protocol](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6455) and [WebSocket API](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/web-sockets.html) are standardized by the W3C and the IETF, and support across browsers is widespread.

### [Read more: WebSockets Alternatives](https://www.pubnub.com/blog/websockets-alternatives-for-realtime-communication/)

## Drawbacks of Web Sockets

-   **Browser Support**: Although most modern browsers support WebSockets, some older ones do not. This can limit the reach of your application and require additional fallback mechanisms for older browsers.
-   **Proxy and Firewall Limitations**: Some proxy servers and firewalls may block or interfere with WebSocket connections. This can cause connectivity issues, especially in secured corporate or restricted network environments.
-   **Scalability**: Web Sockets maintain a persistent connection between the client and the server, which can strain server resources when dealing with many concurrent connections. Proper l[oad balancing](https://www.pubnub.com/guides/load-balancing/) and resource management techniques must be implemented to ensure scalability. Open-source resources, like Socket.io, are not great for large-scale operations or quick growth.
-   **Stateful Nature**: Unlike HTTP, which is stateless, WebSockets are stateful. This means that the server needs to maintain the connection state for each client, leading to increased memory usage and potential scalability challenges.
-   **Security Considerations**: With the persistent connection established by WebSockets, there is a need for proper security measures to protect against potential vulnerabilities, such as cross-site scripting (XSS) and [cross-site request forgery](https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/csrf) (CSRF). Secure WebSocket connections (wss://) using [SSL/TLS](https://www.pubnub.com/guides/ssl-tls/) encryption should be implemented to ensure data privacy and integrity.
-   **Connection Disadvantage**: If a connection over Web Sockets is lost, there are **no included load balancing or reconnecting mechanisms**.
-   It is **still necessary to have fallback options**, like [HTTP streaming](https://www.pubnub.com/guides/what-is-http-streaming/) or long polling, in environments where Web Sockets may not be supported.
-   Features like [Presence](https://www.pubnub.com/docs/sdks/redux/api-reference/presence) do not work well over WebSocket connections because disconnections are hard to detect.

## **WebSockets place in tech stack**

WebSockets fit into the **communication layer** of a [technology stack](https://www.pubnub.com/blog/comprehensive-guide-realtime-tech-stack/). On the server-side, WebSockets are integrated into [backend](https://www.pubnub.com/learn/glossary/backend/) frameworks (e.g., [Node.js](https://www.pubnub.com/blog/nodejs-websocket-programming-examples/), Spring, Django) to [manage connections](https://www.pubnub.com/learn/glossary/connection-management/). WebSockets pass through [load balancers](https://www.pubnub.com/gui

# WebSockets vs SSE vs Long Polling: Comparative Analysis

## Executive Summary Table

| Factor | WebSockets | Server-Sent Events (SSE) | Long Polling |
|--------|------------|---------------------------|--------------|
| **Direction** | Full-duplex (bidirectional) | Server→Client only | Request-response (emulated push) |
| **Protocol** | `ws://` / `wss://` (TCP) | HTTP with `text/event-stream` | Standard HTTP |
| **Connection** | Persistent single connection | Persistent HTTP connection | Repeated HTTP requests |
| **Overhead** | Minimal after handshake | Low (HTTP headers once) | High (headers per request) |
| **Latency** | Lowest (~ms) | Low | Higher (100ms-seconds) |
| **Browser Support** | Excellent (IE10+) | Good (all modern) | Universal |

---

## 1. Pros and Cons

### WebSockets ✅ ❌

| Pros | Cons |
|------|------|
| **Full-duplex communication** — client and server can send simultaneously | **Firewall issues** — some enterprise firewalls (SophosXG, WatchGuard, McAfee) block WebSockets |
| **Lowest latency** — single persistent connection eliminates handshake overhead | **No built-in reconnection** — requires custom implementation or libraries like Socket.IO |
| **Binary + text support** — handles images, video, binary protocols | **Stateful connections** — harder to scale; server must maintain connection state |
| **High throughput** — framed messages, no HTTP header repetition | **No automatic fallback** — must implement or use polyfills |
| **Resource efficient** — one TCP connection per client | **Connection limits** — browser caps concurrent connections per domain (~6-30 depending on impl) |

### Server-Sent Events (SSE) ✅ ❌

| Pros | Cons |
|------|------|
| **Simple HTTP-based** — works through proxies, firewalls, CDNs | **Unidirectional only** — client→server requires separate HTTP requests |
| **Native browser API** (`EventSource`) — no library needed | **No binary support** — text/UTF-8 only (Base64 encode for binary) |
| **Automatic reconnection** — browser handles connection drops | **Six-connection limit** — browsers limit concurrent HTTP connections per domain |
| **HTTP/2 compatible** — multiplexes well | **No IE support** (though IE is deprecated) |
| **Lower server complexity** than WebSockets | **Less efficient for high-frequency bidirectional traffic** |

### Long Polling ✅ ❌

| Pros | Cons |
|------|------|
| **Universal compatibility** — works with any HTTP client/proxy | **High latency** — average delay = timeout/2 |
| **No special server requirements** — standard HTTP endpoints | **Resource intensive** — server threads held open waiting |
| **Simple to implement client-side** | **No message ordering guarantee** — messages can arrive out of order |
| **Works behind strict firewalls** | **Scalability bottleneck** — threads/connections pile up |
| **No connection upgrade needed** | **Connection churn** — frequent connect/disconnect cycles |

---

## 2. Best Use Cases

### WebSockets Are Best For:
- **Real-time chat applications** — bidirectional, low-latency messaging
- **Multiplayer games** — fast state synchronization, player inputs
- **Collaborative editing** (Google Docs-style) — simultaneous changes
- **Financial trading platforms** — sub-second price updates + order entry
- **IoT bidirectional control** — sending commands to devices, receiving telemetry
- **Web-based terminal/SSH clients** — interactive shell sessions

### SSE Is Best For:
- **Live news feeds / social media streams** — server pushes updates
- **Stock price tickers** — one-way streaming data
- **Server monitoring dashboards** — metrics streaming
- **Progress bars for long operations** — server reports job status
- **Live sports scores** — push updates to many clients
- **Log streaming** — tail -f style server logs

### Long Polling Is Best For:
- **Legacy browser support** (IE6-9) — environments without WebSocket/EventSource
- **Strict corporate networks** — where WebSockets are blocked
- **Quick prototypes** — minimal server changes needed
- **Fallback mechanism** — degradation path when "better" options fail
- **Legacy system integration** — existing HTTP infrastructure

---

## 3. Decision Framework: When to Choose Each

```
START
│
├─ Need client→server streaming? ──► YES ──┐
│                                           │
└─ NO ──► Server-Sent Events (or consider Long Polling for legacy)

├─ Need bidirectional + lowest latency? ──► YES ──┐
│                                                 │
└─ NO ──► Consider HTTP/2 Server Push or polling

├─ Behind restrictive firewalls/proxies? ──► YES ──┐
│                                                  │
└─ NO ──► WebSockets

├─ Environment very constrained? ──► YES ───────┐
│                                                 │
└─ NO ──► WebSockets with retry logic

└──► FINAL: Long Polling with exponential backoff
                         ▲
         (WebSockets w/ fallback to SSE or Long Polling)
```

### Guidelines:
- **Default to WebSockets** for real-time bidirectional apps (if infrastructure supports)
- **Use SSE** for server→client streaming where bidirectional isn't needed
- **Keep Long Polling** only as fallback or for extreme legacy constraints
- **Hybrid approach**: Start with WebSockets, degrade to SSE → Long Polling → regular polling

---

## 4. Resource Usage Comparison

| Resource | WebSockets | SSE | Long Polling |
|----------|-----------|-----|--------------|
| **Memory/connection** | ~1-10KB | ~2-5KB | ~50-200KB (per held request) |
| **CPU overhead** | Low (framed messages) | Very low | High (thread pool exhaustion) |
| **Network overhead** | ~2-14 bytes framing | HTTP headers once, then minimal | Full HTTP request/response per message |
| **Concurrent connections** | 10K-1M+ possible | Similar to WebSockets | Typically 1K-10K (limited by threads) |
| **Connection lifetime** | Minutes-hours | Minutes-hours | Seconds (timeout-based) |

### Key Insights:
- **WebSockets**: Most efficient for sustained high-throughput; initial handshake overhead amortized
- **SSE**: Similar efficiency for one-way streaming; no framing overhead
- **Long Polling**: C10K problem — each connection consumes a server thread; thread pools exhaust quickly

---

## 5. Scalability Considerations

### WebSockets Scaling Challenges:
- **Stateful connections** require sticky sessions or shared state (Redis pub/sub)
- **Horizontal scaling**: Need load balancer with session affinity (or use shared message bus)
- **Connection limits**: OS file descriptors, memory per connection
- **Best practices**: 
  - Use connectionless design where possible
  - Implement heartbeat/ping-pong to detect dead connections
  - Use specialized servers (uWebSockets, SocketCluster) over Node.js for high scale

### SSE Scaling Challenges:
- **HTTP connection limits**: Browsers limit ~6 concurrent connections per domain (round-robin or increase via HTTP/2)
- **Proxy timeouts**: CDNs/proxies may cut idle connections at 30-60s
- **Mitigation**: Set `Keep-Alive` headers, implement reconnection logic
- **Advantage**: Works with HTTP load balancers without special configuration

### Long Polling Scaling Challenges:
- **Thread exhaustion**: Each waiting request ties up a thread
- **Solution**: Async servers (Node.js, asyncio, Netty) — but still resource-heavy
- **Load balancer friendly**: No special requirements
- **Mitigation**: Short timeouts (30s), aggressive connection recycling

---

## 6. Fallback Strategies & Compatibility

### Recommended Fallback Chain:
```
1. WebSocket (wss://)
   ↓ (if blocked/fails)
2. WebSocket (ws://) [unencrypted fallback]
   ↓ (if fails)
3. HTTP/2 with Server Push
   ↓ (if unavailable)
4. Server-Sent Events (EventSource)
   ↓ (if unavailable)
5. Long Polling (XHR)
   ↓ (if unavailable)
6. Regular Polling (1-5s intervals)
```

### Implementation Notes:

| Fallback | Library Support | Notes |
|----------|-----------------|-------|
| WebSockets→SSE | Socket.IO, Ably, Pusher | Automatic in managed services |
| SSE→Long Polling | EventSource polyfills | Yaffle's polyfill is popular |
| All→Polling | Socket.IO | Built-in degradation |

### Browser Compatibility:
- **WebSockets**: IE10+, all modern browsers
- **SSE**: IE11+, all modern browsers (polyfills available for IE)
- **Long Polling**: Universal — works with XMLHttpRequest

---

## 7. Quick Reference Decision Matrix

| Requirement | Best Choice |
|-------------|-------------|
| Bidirectional + low latency | **WebSockets** |
| Server push, simple client | **SSE** |
| Maximum compatibility | **Long Polling** |
| Binary data streaming | **WebSockets** |
| Mobile app with battery concerns | **SSE** (lower overhead) |
| Behind corporate proxy/firewall | **SSE or Long Polling** |
| 1M+ concurrent connections | **WebSockets** (with proper architecture) |
| Quick MVP/legacy integration | **Long Polling** |

---

## Sources
- MDN WebSocket API & Server-Sent Events documentation
- Ably Realtime protocol comparison guides
- PubNub WebSocket implementation guides
- Web.dev WebSocketStream API documentation

**Recommendation**: For most new applications requiring real-time features, start with **WebSockets** (using a library like Socket.IO with built-in fallbacks), and use **SSE** specifically for unidirectional server push scenarios where WebSocket complexity isn't justified.

Website
[burjkhalifa.ae](https://www.burjkhalifa.ae/)
References
[[1]](#cite_note-1)

/wiki/File:Burj_Khalifa_(worlds_tallest_building)_and_the_Dubai_skyline_(25781049892).jpgPhotographic [silhouette](/wiki/Silhouette) of the [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai) [skyline](/wiki/Skyline); the Burj Khalifa is visible at the center
The Burj Khalifa[[a]](#cite_note-2) (previously known as Burj Dubai prior to inauguration) is a [megatall](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers) [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) in [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai), United Arab Emirates. Designed by [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill), it is the [world's tallest structure](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures_in_the_world), with a total height of 829.8 m (2,722 ft, or just over half a mile) and a roof height (excluding the antenna, but including a 242.6 m spire)[[2]](#cite_note-Vanity-3) of 828 m (2,717 ft). It has also been the [tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_and_structures) in the world since its [topping out](/wiki/Topping_out) in 2009, surpassing [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101), which had held the record for a half-decade.[[3]](#cite_note-CTBUHdb-4)[[4]](#cite_note-DubaiOneInauguration-5)

Construction of the Burj Khalifa began in 2004; the exterior was completed five years later. The primary structure is [reinforced concrete](/wiki/Reinforced_concrete). Some of the [structural steel](/wiki/Structural_steel) for the building was salvaged from the demolished [Palace of the Republic](/wiki/Palace_of_the_Republic,_Berlin) in East Berlin.[[5]](#cite_note-dw-6) The building was opened in 2010 as part of a new development called [Downtown Dubai](/wiki/Downtown_Dubai). It was designed to be the centerpiece of large-scale, mixed-use development.

The building is named after the former president of the United Arab Emirates (UAE), Sheikh [Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan](/wiki/Khalifa_bin_Zayed_Al_Nahyan).[[6]](#cite_note-USAtoday-7) The [United Arab Emirates government](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_government) provided Dubai with financial support as the developer, [Emaar Properties](/wiki/Emaar_Properties), experienced financial problems during the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession). Then-president of the United Arab Emirates, Khalifa bin Zayed, organized federal financial support. For his support, [Mohammad bin Rashid](/wiki/Mohammad_bin_Rashid), [Ruler of Dubai](/wiki/Ruler_of_Dubai), changed the name from "Burj Dubai" to "Burj Khalifa" during inauguration.

The design is derived from the [Islamic architecture](/wiki/Islamic_architecture) of the region, such as in the [Great Mosque of Samarra](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Samarra). The Y-shaped tripartite floor geometry is designed to optimise residential and hotel space. A [buttressed central core and wings](/wiki/Buttressed_core) are used to support the height of the building. The Burj Khalifa's central core houses all vertical transportation except egress stairs within each of the wings.[[7]](#cite_note-academic.csuohio.edu-8) The structure also features a [cladding](/wiki/Cladding_(construction)) system which is designed to withstand Dubai's hot summer temperatures.[[8]](#cite_note-9) It contains a total of 57 elevators and 8 escalators.

## Development

Construction began on 12 January 2004, with the exterior of the structure completed on 1 October 2009. The building officially opened on 4 January 2010[[9]](#cite_note-opening-10)[[10]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-11) and is part of the 2 km2 (490 acres) [Downtown Dubai](/wiki/Downtown_Dubai) development at the 'First Interchange' along [Sheikh Zayed Road](/wiki/Sheikh_Zayed_Road), near Dubai's main business district.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

The tower's architecture and engineering were performed by [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill) of Chicago, with [Adrian Smith](/wiki/Adrian_Smith_(architect)) as chief architect, and [Bill Baker](/wiki/William_F._Baker_(engineer)) as a chief structural engineer.[[11]](#cite_note-Record_High-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13) The firm had designed the [Sears Tower](/wiki/Willis_Tower) in Chicago, a previous record holder for the world's tallest building.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

[Hyder Consulting](/wiki/Hyder_Consulting) was supervising engineer and NORR Group Consultants supervised the architecture. The primary contractor was [Samsung C&T](/wiki/Samsung_C%26T) of [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea), together with the Belgian group [BESIX](/wiki/Besix) and the local company [Arabtec](/wiki/Arabtec_Holding_PJSC).

Numerous complaints concerned migrant workers from [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia), the primary building labour force, who were paid low wages and sometimes had their passports confiscated.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

## Conception

Burj Khalifa was designed to be the centerpiece of a large-scale, mixed-use development to include 30,000 homes,[[16]](#cite_note-17) nine hotels (including [The Address Downtown Dubai](/wiki/The_Address_Downtown_Dubai)), 3 hectares (7.4 acres) of parkland, at least 19 residential skyscrapers, the [Dubai Mall](/wiki/Dubai_Mall), and the 12-hectare (30-acre) artificial Burj Khalifa Lake. The decision to build Burj Khalifa was reportedly based on the government's decision to diversify from an oil-based economy to one that is service and

  "cached": true
}

[[3]](#cite_note-Emporis-3)

Merdeka 118, formerly known as Menara Warisan Merdeka,[[a]](#cite_note-13) KL 118, and PNB 118, is a 118-story [megatall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers) in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur), Malaysia. At 678.9 metres (2,227 feet) tall,[[1]](#cite_note-Merdeka_118-1) it is the [second-tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) and [structure](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures) in the world, only behind the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) (Dubai, UAE), at 829.8 m (2,722 ft). Construction was controversially entirely funded by [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad), an investment management company [owned by the Malaysian government](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise).[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14) The structure was completed in November of 2023, celebrated its grand opening on 10 January 2024, but remains closed and under further construction as of 1 July 2025.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)] The Park Hyatt Kuala Lumpur Hotel, located on level 75, began operation on 7 August 2025.[[14]](#cite_note-parkhyatt-15)

The building's name, Merdeka, which means "independence" in [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language), is inspired by its proximity to [Stadium Merdeka](/wiki/Stadium_Merdeka).[[15]](#cite_note-future-16) The spire of the building was completed in December 2022, which marked its final height of 678.9 m (2,227 ft) above ground and 700.9 m (2,300 ft) above sea level.[[16]](#cite_note-:0-17)

It is the [tallest building in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Malaysia)—surpassing the 451.9-metre (1,483 ft) [Petronas Twin Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers)—and in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Southeast_Asia), surpassing the 462-metre (1,516 ft) [Landmark 81](/wiki/Landmark_81).[[17]](#cite_note-:1-18) The building will also be the first in Malaysia to receive a triple platinum rating from worldwide sustainability certifications such as Green Building Index (GBI), including [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design).[[17]](#cite_note-:1-18)

When it was completed in 2023,[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) the tower became the tallest building in Malaysia.[[20]](#cite_note-21) It was constructed in three phases and consists of 400,000 square metres (4,300,000 sq ft) of residential, hotel and commercial space.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

The building is a mix of office spaces, hotels, and retail outlets and an observatory floor which will be the highest observation deck in Southeast Asia. It has two observation decks, the first inside the building and the second inside the spire, and a retail business center ([118 Mall](/wiki/118_Mall)). It will be surrounded by four acres of urban and linear parks.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The non-rentable space consists of elevators, recreational and maintenance facilities, as well as parking spaces for up to 8,500 cars. Sixty out of the 80 storeys of office space will be reserved for [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad) (PNB), the developer of the project, and its subsidiaries.[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The Merdeka 118 precinct is a 7.7-hectare (19-acre) land development funded by [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad) (PNB),[[25]](#cite_note-26) a [state-owned enterprise](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise) of the Malaysian government.[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14)

The budget of RM5 billion[[26]](#cite_note-or_$1,134,429,948.95_(USD):2-27) has faced criticism from many Malaysians, saying that the money was in essence public money and could have been better used elsewhere, such as for basic infrastructure and a raise in the minimum wage.[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14) In 2016, [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia) [Najib Razak](/wiki/Najib_Razak), who would eventually be directly involved with the [1MDB](/wiki/1Malaysia_Development_Berhad) scandal, claimed that the project was not a waste and it would "bring more benefits" by generating "econo
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="a37c445ba1c55749">>>",
  "cached": true
}

[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system)[3°08′30″N 101°42′03″E / 3.14167°N 101.70072°E](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Merdeka_118&params=3.14167_N_101.70072_E_type:landmark_region:MY)
Named for[Malaysian Independence](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Malaysia))

[[3]](#cite_note-Emporis-3)

Merdeka 118, formerly known as Menara Warisan Merdeka,[[a]](#cite_note-13) KL 118, and PNB 118, is a 118-story [megatall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers) in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur), Malaysia. At 678.9 metres (2,227 feet) tall,[[1]](#cite_note-Merdeka_118-1) it is the [second-tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) and [structure](/wiki/List_of_tallest_structures) in the world, only behind the [Burj Khalifa](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) (Dubai, UAE), at 829.8 m (2,722 ft). Construction was controversially entirely funded by [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad), an investment management company [owned by the Malaysian government](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise).[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14) The structure was completed in November of 2023, celebrated its grand opening on 10 January 2024, but remains closed and under further construction as of 1 July 2025.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)] The Park Hyatt Kuala Lumpur Hotel, located on level 75, began operation on 7 August 2025.[[14]](#cite_note-parkhyatt-15)

The building's name, Merdeka, which means "independence" in [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language), is inspired by its proximity to [Stadium Merdeka](/wiki/Stadium_Merdeka).[[15]](#cite_note-future-16) The spire of the building was completed in December 2022, which marked its final height of 678.9 m (2,227 ft) above ground and 700.9 m (2,300 ft) above sea level.[[16]](#cite_note-:0-17)

It is the [tallest building in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Malaysia)—surpassing the 451.9-metre (1,483 ft) [Petronas Twin Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers)—and in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Southeast_Asia), surpassing the 462-metre (1,516 ft) [Landmark 81](/wiki/Landmark_81).[[17]](#cite_note-:1-18) The building will also be the first in Malaysia to receive a triple platinum rating from worldwide sustainability certifications such as Green Building Index (GBI), including [LEED](/wiki/Leadership_in_Energy_and_Environmental_Design).[[17]](#cite_note-:1-18)

When it was completed in 2023,[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) the tower became the tallest building in Malaysia.[[20]](#cite_note-21) It was constructed in three phases and consists of 400,000 square metres (4,300,000 sq ft) of residential, hotel and commercial space.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

The building is a mix of office spaces, hotels, and retail outlets and an observatory floor which will be the highest observation deck in Southeast Asia. It has two observation decks, the first inside the building and the second inside the spire, and a retail business center ([118 Mall](/wiki/118_Mall)). It will be surrounded by four acres of urban and linear parks.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The non-rentable space consists of elevators, recreational and maintenance facilities, as well as parking spaces for up to 8,500 cars. Sixty out of the 80 storeys of office space will be reserved for [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad) (PNB), the developer of the project, and its subsidiaries.[[23]](#cite_note-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The Merdeka 118 precinct is a 7.7-hectare (19-acre) land development funded by [Permodalan Nasional Berhad](/wiki/Permodalan_Nasional_Berhad) (PNB),[[25]](#cite_note-26) a [state-owned enterprise](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise) of the Malaysian government.[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14)

The budget of RM5 billion[[26]](#cite_note-or_$1,134,429,948.95_(USD):2-27) has faced criticism from many Malaysians, saying that the money was in essence public money and could have been better used elsewhere, such as for basic infrastructure and a raise in the minimum wage.[[13]](#cite_note-VI-14) In 2016, [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia) [Najib Razak](/wiki/Najib_Razak), who would eventually be directly involved with the [1MDB](/wiki/1Malaysia_Development_Berhad) scandal, claimed that the project was not a waste and it would "bring more benefits" by generating "econo
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="a37c445ba1c55749">>>

References
[[1]](#cite_note-OwnersWSJ-1)[[2]](#cite_note-skyscraperCentre-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-gensler-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Forbes2013-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FT2013N-6)[[7]](#cite_note-LATimesVideo-7)

The Shanghai Tower[[a]](#cite_note-9) is a 128-story, 632-meter-tall (2,073-foot) [megatall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_megatall_skyscrapers) located in [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui), [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong), [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai).[[9]](#cite_note-uc-10) It is currently the [tallest building in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China), and the world's [third-tallest building by height to architectural top](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings). It was the tallest and largest [LEED](/wiki/LEED)-CS Platinum certified building in the world from 2015 to 2024. It was also the second tallest-building in the world, from 2015 to 2021, until the completion of [Merdeka 118](/wiki/Merdeka_118) in Malaysia. The Shanghai Tower also had the world's fastest [elevators](/wiki/Elevator) at a top speed of 20.5 meters per second (74 kilometers per hour; 46 miles per hour) until 2017,[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12) when it was surpassed by the [Guangzhou CTF Finance Center](/wiki/Guangzhou_CTF_Finance_Center), with its top speed of 21 meters per second (76 km/h; 47 mph).[[12]](#cite_note-13)

Designed by the international design firm [Gensler](/wiki/Gensler) and owned by the Shanghai Municipal Government,[[1]](#cite_note-OwnersWSJ-1) it is the tallest of the world's first triple-adjacent supertall buildings in [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong), the other two being the [Jin Mao Tower](/wiki/Jin_Mao_Tower) and the [Shanghai World Financial Center](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center). Its tiered construction, designed for high energy efficiency, provides nine separate zones divided between office, retail and leisure use.[[4]](#cite_note-gensler-4)[[6]](#cite_note-FT2013N-6)[[13]](#cite_note-:1-14) The US-based [Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat](/wiki/Council_on_Tall_Buildings_and_Urban_Habitat) cites it as "one of the most sustainably advanced tall buildings in the world."[[14]](#cite_note-15)

Groundbreaking and construction work on the tower began on 29 November 2008 and [topped out](/wiki/Topping_out) on 4 August 2013.[[9]](#cite_note-uc-10)[[15]](#cite_note-16) The exterior was completed in summer 2015,[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[13]](#cite_note-:1-14) and work was considered complete in September 2014. Although the building was originally scheduled to open to the public in November 2014, the actual public-use date was shifted to February 2015. The [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) was opened to visitors in July 2016; the period from July through September 2018 was termed a "test run" or "commissioning" period.[[17]](#cite_note-:3-18)[[18]](#cite_note-:4-19)

Since 26 April 2017, the sightseeing decks on the 118th and 119th floors (546 m and 552 m high respectively) have been fully open to the public.[[19]](#cite_note-20) By 2020, the opening of a further deck, dubbed the "Top of Shanghai" on the 121st floor at 562 m (1844 ft), made it the highest observation deck in the world, beating out the [Burj Khalifa's](/wiki/Burj_Khalifa) observation deck at 555 m (1823 ft).[[20]](#cite_note-21) The J Hotel Shanghai Tower, opened on the 120th floor in 2021, became the world's highest luxury hotel by height [above ground level](/wiki/Height_above_ground_level).[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)

### Planning and funding
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shanghai_Tower&action=edit&section=2)]

Planning models for the [Lujiazui](/wiki/Lujiazui) financial district dating back to 1993 show plans for a close group of three supertall skyscrapers.[[23]](#cite_note-24) The first of these, the [Jin Mao Tower](/wiki/Jin_Mao_Tower), was completed in 1999; the adjacent [Shanghai World Financial Center](/wiki/Shanghai_World_Financial_Center) (SWFC) opened in 2008.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

The Shanghai Tower is owned by Yeti Construction and Development, a consortium of state-owned development companies which includes Shanghai Chengtou Corp., Shanghai Lujiazui Finance & Trade Zone Development Co., and [Shanghai Construction Group](/wiki/Shanghai_Construction_Group).[[1]](#cite_note-OwnersWSJ-1)[[25]](#cite_note-:2-26) Funding for the tower's construction was obtained from [shareholders](/wiki/Shareholder), bank loans, and Shanghai's municipal government.[[26]](#cite_note-27) The tower had an estimated construction cost of US$2.4 billion.[[6]](#cite_note-FT2013N-6)

In 2008, the site – previously a [driving range](/wiki/Driving_range)[[27]](#cite_note-28) – was prepared for construction.[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30) A groundbreaking ceremony was held on 29 November 2008, after the tower had passed an [environmental impact study](/wiki/Environmental_impact_study).[[30]](#cite_note-31) The main construction contractor for the project was [Shanghai Construction Group](/wiki/Shanghai_Construction_Group), a member of the consortium that owns the tower.[[25]](#cite_note-:2-26)

A repetitive slip-forming process was used to construct the tower's core floor by floor.[[31]](#cite_note-GenOn-32) By late April 2011, the tower's steel reinforcement had risen to the 18th floor, while its concrete core had reached the 15th floor, and floor framing had been completed up to the fourth floor.[[31]](#cite_note-GenOn-32) By late December 2011, the tower's foundations had been completed, and its steel construction had risen above the 30th floor.[[32]](#cite_note-33) In the first months of 2012, cracks began appearing in the roads near the tower's construction site. These were blamed on ground [subsidence](/wiki/Subsidence), which was likely cau
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="2f3f4de30d0d010e">>>",
  "cached": true
}

The Clock Towers ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language): أبراج الساعة, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic): ʾAbrāj as-Sāʿaẗ, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation) 'Towers of the Clock', formerly known as Abraj al-Bait [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language): أبراج البيت, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Arabic): ʾAbrāj al-Bayt, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation) 'Towers of the House') is a [government-owned](/wiki/State_ownership) hotel complex with seven [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) [hotels](/wiki/Hotel) in [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca), [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia). These towers are part of the [King Abdulaziz Endowment Project](/wiki/Abdullah_of_Saudi_Arabia#Domestic_affairs) that aims to modernize the city in catering to its pilgrims. The central hotel tower, which is the Mecca Royal Clock Tower, is the fourth-[tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings) and sixth-[tallest freestanding structure in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_freestanding_structures).[[7]](#cite_note-7) According to [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records), the Mecca Royal Clock Tower is the tallest clock tower in the world, and the complex of seven buildings comprise the world's second most expensive building.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The clock faces are the [largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_clock_faces), and the top four floors of the clock tower house the [Clock Tower Museum](/wiki/Clock_Tower_Museum).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The building complex is 300 metres (980 ft) away from the world's largest [mosque](/wiki/Mosque) and [Islam](/wiki/Islam)'s [most sacred site](/wiki/Holiest_sites_in_Islam), the [Great Mosque of Mecca](/wiki/Great_Mosque_of_Mecca).[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) The developer and contractor of the complex is the [Saudi Binladin Group](/wiki/Saudi_Binladin_Group), the Kingdom's largest [construction company](/wiki/Construction_company). The total cost of construction totaled US$15 billion. The complex was built after the demolition of the [Ajyad Fortress](/wiki/Ajyad_Fortress), the 18th-century [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire) citadel on top of a hill overlooking the Grand Mosque.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The [destruction of the historically significant site](/wiki/Destruction_of_early_Islamic_heritage_sites_in_Saudi_Arabia) in 2002 by the [Saudi government](/wiki/Saudi_government) sparked an outcry and a strong reaction from Turkey.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

/wiki/File:Abraj_al_bait_Clock_3.jpgThe Mecca Royal Clock Tower's, also known as Massive Mohammad after [Big Ben](/wiki/Big_Ben)'s clocks, the largest in the world
The tallest tower in the complex is the [tallest building in Saudi Arabia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Saudi_Arabia), at 601 meters (1,972 feet) and a total surface area of 32,000 square meters. It is the sixth-tallest freestanding structure in the world, surpassing the [Ping An Finance Centre](/wiki/Ping_An_F
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="2e0ef1194b801f56">>>"

Chinese name

[Traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters)平安金融中心
Literal meaningPing An Finance Centre
Transcriptions
[Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese)
[Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin)Píng'ān Jīnróng Zhōngxīn
[Yue: Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese)
[Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping)ping4ngon1 gam1jung4 zung1sam1

The Ping An Finance Center ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language): 平安金融中心; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Píng'ān Jīnróng Zhōngxīn) is a 115-[storey](/wiki/Storey), 599.1-metre (1,966-foot) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/Supertall) in [Shenzhen, Guangdong](/wiki/Shenzhen,_Guangdong), China.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It was completed in 2017,[[1]](#cite_note-CTBUH-1) and is the [tallest building in Shenzhen](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Shenzhen), the [2nd tallest building in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China) (after the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower)), and the [5th tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_world).[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3) The building contains office, hotel and retail spaces, a conference center, and a high-end shopping mall.

The [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) is named Free Sky and is located on floor 116,[[9]](#cite_note-thetowerinfo-9) which broke the record of having the highest observation deck in a building at 562.5 m (1,845 ft).[[10]](#cite_note-ctbuh.org-10) The observation deck allows 360-degree views. Inside there are a transparent viewing area, interactive experience area, and special display area to showcase youth cultures with Shenzhen special characteristics through multimedia facilities. The opening hours are 10am to 8pm, last entry 7.15pm.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The building houses the headquarters of [Ping An Insurance](/wiki/Ping_An_Insurance), with their company name representing the building name. The building was commissioned by Ping An Insurance and designed by the American architectural firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox_Associates). The design of the building is meant to be unique and elegant, and to represent the history and achievements of the main tenant. A stainless-steel facade that weighs approximately 1,700 metric tons (3,700,000 lb) provides a modern design to the building.[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3)

It is the second largest skyscraper in the world by floor area after [Azabudai Hills Main Tower](/wiki/Azabudai_Hills) in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo), Japan. The building has a gross floor area of 378,600 square metres (4,075,000 square feet). The 115-storey tower has a width-to-height aspect ratio of 1:10 and also has an 11-story podium. Including the podium, the building has 495,520 m2 (5,333,700 sq ft) of floor space. A five-level basement adds 90,000 m2 (970,000 sq ft). The 620,000-metric-ton (1.37×109 lb; 620,000,000 kg) tower has eight main columns which form the superstructure. The column dimensions range from approximately 6 by 3.2 m (19 ft 8 in by 10 ft 6 in) at the lowest level to 2.9 by 1.4 m (9 ft 6 in by 4 ft 7 in) at the top of the tower.[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3)

The second building of the project, a 290-metre (950 ft), 4
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="815278f56bda56da">>>"

The Ping An Finance Center ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language): 平安金融中心; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Píng'ān Jīnróng Zhōngxīn) is a 115-[storey](/wiki/Storey), 599.1-metre (1,966-foot) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/Supertall) in [Shenzhen, Guangdong](/wiki/Shenzhen,_Guangdong), China.[[7]](#cite_note-7) It was completed in 2017,[[1]](#cite_note-CTBUH-1) and is the [tallest building in Shenzhen](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Shenzhen), the [2nd tallest building in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China) (after the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower)), and the [5th tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_world).[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3) The building contains office, hotel and retail spaces, a conference center, and a high-end shopping mall.

The [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) is named Free Sky and is located on floor 116,[[9]](#cite_note-thetowerinfo-9) which broke the record of having the highest observation deck in a building at 562.5 m (1,845 ft).[[10]](#cite_note-ctbuh.org-10) The observation deck allows 360-degree views. Inside there are a transparent viewing area, interactive experience area, and special display area to showcase youth cultures with Shenzhen special characteristics through multimedia facilities. The opening hours are 10am to 8pm, last entry 7.15pm.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The building houses the headquarters of [Ping An Insurance](/wiki/Ping_An_Insurance), with their company name representing the building name. The building was commissioned by Ping An Insurance and designed by the American architectural firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox_Associates). The design of the building is meant to be unique and elegant, and to represent the history and achievements of the main tenant. A stainless-steel facade that weighs approximately 1,700 metric tons (3,700,000 lb) provides a modern design to the building.[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3)

It is the second largest skyscraper in the world by floor area after [Azabudai Hills Main Tower](/wiki/Azabudai_Hills) in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo), Japan. The building has a gross floor area of 378,600 square metres (4,075,000 square feet). The 115-storey tower has a width-to-height aspect ratio of 1:10 and also has an 11-story podium. Including the podium, the building has 495,520 m2 (5,333,700 sq ft) of floor space. A five-level basement adds 90,000 m2 (970,000 sq ft). The 620,000-metric-ton (1.37×109 lb; 620,000,000 kg) tower has eight main columns which form the superstructure. The column dimensions range from approximately 6 by 3.2 m (19 ft 8 in by 10 ft 6 in) at the lowest level to 2.9 by 1.4 m (9 ft 6 in by 4 ft 7 in) at the top of the tower.[[3]](#cite_note-ASCE_Dec2017-3)

The second building of the project, a 290-metre (950 ft), 4
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="815278f56bda56da">>>

Lotte World Tower ([Korean](/wiki/Korean_language): 롯데월드타워; [RR](/wiki/Revised_Romanization_of_Korean): lotdewoldeutawo) is a 123-story, 555 metres (1,821 ft; 21,900 in) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers), located in [Sincheon-dong](/wiki/Sincheon-dong,_Seoul), [Songpa District](/wiki/Songpa_District), [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea). It is [the sixth-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings), the tallest in [OECD](/wiki/OECD) countries and [in South Korea](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_South_Korea), and also the first in South Korea to be over 100 stories tall. It was the fifth-tallest in the world when completed.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The tower opened to the public on April 3, 2017.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

A "Sky Bridge Tour" is located on the roof of Lotte World Tower at 541 m (1,775 ft),[[6]](#cite_note-6) which is about the same height of [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center) in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City) (1,776 feet or 541 meters), the [tallest building in the United States](/wiki/Tallest_building_in_the_United_States).

After 13 years of planning and site preparation,[[7]](#cite_note-world.kbs.co.kr-7) the tower gained final approval to start construction by the government in November 2010[[7]](#cite_note-world.kbs.co.kr-7) and the first groundbreaking activities of piling and frame assembly were observed at the construction site in March 2012.

On December 31, 2015, the [LED](/wiki/LED)-[pixels](/wiki/Pixel) of the façade displayed the number "2016".[[8]](#cite_note-8)

On April 3, 2017, Lotte shot off fireworks to celebrate the tower's official opening.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Fireworks display of the Lotte World Tower's grand opening
On January 1, 2018, Lotte shot off fireworks with a LED laser show for seven minutes to celebrate New Year's Day and the [2018 PyeongChang Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On March 17, 2016, before the final phase of external construction, the [diagrid](/wiki/Diagrid) lantern-shaped roof structure was completed. The roof structure was constructed with steel counterparts that are each 12 m (39 ft) and weigh 20 tonnes (20 long tons; 22 short tons). The counterparts were made up of bent metal panels that are 6 centimetres (2.4 in) thick. The roof structure itself is 120 m (390 ft) high and covers floors 107–123. Approximately 3,000 tonnes (3,000 long tons; 3,300 short tons) of steel parts, a high-precision 64-tonne (63-long-ton; 71-short-ton) tower crane, and [GPS](/wiki/GPS) alignment systems, as well as welding technicians, were used in the construction of the roof.

The roof structure is engineered to withstand its weight without reinforcing pillars, endure earthquakes up to a magnitude of 9 under the [Richter magnitude scale](/wiki/Richter_magnitude_scale), and winds up to 80 m/s (260 ft/s).

The exterior of pale-coloured glass draws inspiration from Korean [ceramics](/wiki/Ceramic_art) and features accents of metal [filigree](/wiki/Filigree).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The tower incorporates several environmental sustainability features, including an advanced heat pump system for heating and cooling, which utilizes 720 geothermal probes installed about 200 meters underground as well as water from the Han River. Additional technologies include heat-insulating glass with passive shading systems, photovoltaic panels, wind turbines
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="bcb8efff157791ee">>>"

One World Trade Center, also known as One WTC and the Freedom Tower,[[note 1]](#cite_note-15) is the main building of the [rebuilt World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(2001%E2%80%93present)) complex in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan), New York City. Designed by [David Childs](/wiki/David_Childs) of [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill), One World Trade Center is the [tallest building in the United States](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_United_States), the tallest [building](/wiki/Skyscraper) in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere), and the [seventh-tallest in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings). The [supertall structure](/wiki/Supertall_structure) has the same name as the [North Tower](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1970%E2%80%932001)) of the [original World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)), which was [destroyed](/wiki/Collapse_of_the_World_Trade_Center) in the [terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001](/wiki/September_11_attacks). The skyscraper stands on the northwest corner of the 16-acre (6.5 ha) [World Trade Center site](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_site), on the site of the original [6 World Trade Center](/wiki/6_World_Trade_Center_(1974%E2%80%932001)). It is bounded by [West Street](/wiki/West_Side_Highway) to the west, [Vesey Street](/wiki/Vesey_Street) to the north, [Fulton Street](/wiki/Fulton_Street_(Manhattan)) to the south, and [Washington Street](/wiki/Washington_Street_(Manhattan)) to the east.

The construction of below-ground utility relocations, footings, and foundations for the new building began on April 27, 2006. One World Trade Center became the [tallest structure in New York City](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_New_York_City) on April 30, 2012, when it surpassed the height of the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building). The tower's steel structure was [topped out](/wiki/Topping_out) on August 30, 2012. On May 10, 2013, after the final component of the skyscraper's [spire](/wiki/Spire) was installed, it reached a height of 1,776 feet (541 m). Its height in feet references the signing of the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence) in 1776. The building opened on November 3, 2014;[[12]](#cite_note-CNN_Money-12) the One World Observatory opened on May 29, 2015.[[13]](#cite_note-U.S._News-13)

On March 26, 2009, the [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey) (PANYNJ) confirmed that the building would be officially known by its legal name of One World Trade Center, rather than its colloquial name of Freedom Tower.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) The building has 94 stories, with the top floor numbered 104.

One World Trade Center, also known as One WTC and the Freedom Tower,[[note 1]](#cite_note-15) is the main building of the [rebuilt World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(2001%E2%80%93present)) complex in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan), New York City. Designed by [David Childs](/wiki/David_Childs) of [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill), One World Trade Center is the [tallest building in the United States](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_United_States), the tallest [building](/wiki/Skyscraper) in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere), and the [seventh-tallest in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings). The [supertall structure](/wiki/Supertall_structure) has the same name as the [North Tower](/wiki/1_World_Trade_Center_(1970%E2%80%932001)) of the [original World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001)), which was [destroyed](/wiki/Collapse_of_the_World_Trade_Center) in the [terrorist attacks of September 11, 2001](/wiki/September_11_attacks). The skyscraper stands on the northwest corner of the 16-acre (6.5 ha) [World Trade Center site](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_site), on the site of the original [6 World Trade Center](/wiki/6_World_Trade_Center_(1974%E2%80%932001)). It is bounded by [West Street](/wiki/West_Side_Highway) to the west, [Vesey Street](/wiki/Vesey_Street) to the north, [Fulton Street](/wiki/Fulton_Street_(Manhattan)) to the south, and [Washington Street](/wiki/Washington_Street_(Manhattan)) to the east.

The construction of below-ground utility relocations, footings, and foundations for the new building began on April 27, 2006. One World Trade Center became the [tallest structure in New York City](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_New_York_City) on April 30, 2012, when it surpassed the height of the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building). The tower's steel structure was [topped out](/wiki/Topping_out) on August 30, 2012. On May 10, 2013, after the final component of the skyscraper's [spire](/wiki/Spire) was installed, it reached a height of 1,776 feet (541 m). Its height in feet references the signing of the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence) in 1776. The building opened on November 3, 2014;[[12]](#cite_note-CNN_Money-12) the One World Observatory opened on May 29, 2015.[[13]](#cite_note-U.S._News-13)

On March 26, 2009, the [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey) (PANYNJ) confirmed that the building would be officially known by its legal name of One World Trade Center, rather than its colloquial name of Freedom Tower.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) The building has 94 stories, with the top floor numbered 104.

The new World Trade Center complex will eventually include five high-rise office buildings built along [Greenwich Street](/wiki/Greenwich_Street), the [National September 11 Memorial & Museum](/wiki/National_September_11_Memorial_%26_Museum), located just south of One World Trade Center where the original Twin Towers stood, and the [World Trade Center Transportation Hub](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_Transportation_Hub) to its east. The construction is part of an effort to memorialize and rebuild following the destruction of the original World Trade Center complex.

The Guangzhou Chow Tai Fook Finance Centre ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters): 广州周大福金融中心; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters): 廣州周大福金融中心; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Guǎngzhōu zhōudàfú jīnróng zhōngxīn), also called East Tower, is a 530-metre (1,739 ft) [mixed-use](/wiki/Mixed-use) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/Supertall_skyscraper) in [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou), [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong), [China](/wiki/China), which was completed in October 2016. It is the [tallest completed building in Guangzhou](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Guangzhou), the [third-tallest in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China), and the [eighth-tallest in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings). The Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre has a total of 111 above ground and five below ground floors and houses a shopping mall, offices, apartments, and a [hotel](/wiki/Hotel). The skyscraper has a [gross floor area](/wiki/Floor_area_(building)) of 507,681.0 square metres (5,464,633 sq ft), of which a little over 20% is not part of the skyscraper itself, but of the podium connected to it.

The Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre is situated on a 27,000-square-metre (290,000 sq ft) lot along Zhujiang East Road in [Zhujiang New Town](/wiki/Zhujiang_New_Town), Guangzhou's [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district).[[6]](#cite_note-:3-6) In that neighbourhood, the skyscraper is located east of the central axis with an underground mall and connections to public transportation underneath it.[[7]](#cite_note-Featured_Tall_Building-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:11-8) The Guangzhou CTF Centre is part of the [Guangzhou Twin Towers](/wiki/Guangzhou_Twin_Towers). The other tower of the pair, the 439-metre (1,439 ft) tall [Guangzhou International Finance Center](/wiki/Guangzhou_International_Finance_Center), is located on the other side of the axis and is also known under the name "West Tower". The Guangzhou CTF Centre is therefore known as the "East Tower" as well.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) Both towers have a similar height, size, and function, and are situated close to the 604 m (1,982 ft) tall [Canton Tower](/wiki/Canton_Tower).[[7]](#cite_note-Featured_Tall_Building-7)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre is owned by [Chow Tai Fook Enterprises](/wiki/Chow_Tai_Fook_Enterprises), and houses the world's fastest elevators, which can reach speeds up to 21 metres per second (69 feet per second).[[10]](#cite_note-:6-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The Guangzhou CTF Finance Centre consists of a skyscraper and an eight-floor podium, that is connected to the skyscraper. The podium features a 30 m (100 ft) high [atrium](/wiki/Atrium_(architecture)) with a glass roof.[[12]](#cite
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="e3f9bb55235eec92">>>"
}

Tianjin CTF Finance Centre
[Simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters)天津周大福滨海中心
[Traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters)天津周大福濱海中心
Transcriptions
[Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese)
[Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin)Tiānjīn Zhōu Dà Fú Bīnhǎi Zhōngxīn

Tianjin CTF Finance Centre is a [supertall](/wiki/Supertall_building) [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) located in the [Tianjin Economic-Technological Development Area](/wiki/Tianjin_Economic-Technological_Development_Area) [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district) of [Binhai](/wiki/Binhai_New_Area), [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin), [China](/wiki/China). Construction started in 2013 and was completed in 2019. At 530 m (1,740 ft), and with 97 floors, the tower is the second-tallest building in Municipal Tianjin after unfinished [Goldin Finance 117](/wiki/Goldin_Finance_117), the [eighth-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings), and the tallest building in the world with fewer than 100 floors.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is located in the outer district of the [Tianjin Economic-Technological Development Area](/wiki/Tianjin_Economic-Technological_Development_Area). Tianjin CTF Finance Center is designed by [Skidmore, Owings & Merrill LLP](/wiki/Skidmore,_Owings_%26_Merrill) in collaboration with [Ronald Lu & Partners](/wiki/Ronald_Lu_%26_Partners).

The building was honored with the Tall/Slender Structure award at the 2021 Awards Ceremony by the Institution of Structural Engineers (IStructE).[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The Tianjin CTF Finance Centre is designed with a gently curving glass facade that incorporates eight sloping mega-columns. The columns follow a line connecting the centers and corners of all four elevations. This design improves the building's seismic resilience and contributes to both its gravity and lateral systems. The columns are effective in increasing the stiffness of the building's perimeter frame, consequently attracting a larger portion of the seismic forces in compliance with the Chinese code requirements.

The façade reinforces the curvature of the tower form and creates a shimmering texture over the building's surface. The crystalline-like curtain wall stretches from the suspended glass canopies at each of the lobbies to the dematerialized, mega-column-looped crown and presents a bold expression of a comprehensive, integrated design on the Tianjin skyline.

/wiki/File:Tianjin_CTF_Finance_Centre,_4_of_24.jpgBase from street level showing concave and convex curves

By stacking reducing floor plates, the tower tapers dramatically to minimize the surface area exposed to wind, sun, and moisture. The gently-undulating curves of the facade subtly denote the integration of the three distinct programs within a singular smooth object. Square in plan with rounded corners, the floor plate geometry enables unique interior fit-outs and customization options for occupants. Research by the architect has shown that lateral forces due to vortex shedding can be controlled by tapering the vertical profile of the tower and softening any sharp corners in plan. The building's aerodynamic shape greatly reduces this vortex shedding by "confusing the wind" and disrupting the opportunity for any resonating wind forces and loads on the structure.

## Construction process
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tianjin_CTF_Finance_Centre&action=edit&section=3)]

- On November 20, 2009, Tianjin Chow Tai Fook Financial Center held a groundbreaking ceremony. The then Tianjin Mayor [Huang Xingguo](/wiki/Huang_Xingguo) attended the ceremony.

- The Tianjin CTF Finance Centre was proposed as an idea in 2011.

- As of April 2016, one third of the entire building had been built.

- In July 2016, the core tube of the building was poured to 260 meters.

- In September 2016, the building was built as a transition truss between the office building and the serviced residential part.

- In April 2017, CTBUH confirmed that the

}

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

CITIC Tower
中国尊

/wiki/File:China_Zun_tillsammans_med_Samsung_China_Headquarters_(januari_2019)_Cropped.jpgCITIC Tower (2019)

Interactive map of the CITIC Tower area

Alternative namesChina Zun, Zhongguo Zun
General information
StatusCompleted
Architectural style[Modern](/wiki/Modern_architecture)
LocationZ15 plot, Guanghua Road, [Beijing CBD](/wiki/Beijing_CBD), [Chaoyang District](/wiki/Chaoyang_District,_Beijing), [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing), [China](/wiki/China)
Groundbreaking19 September 2011
Construction started15 May 2012
Completed30 September 2018
Height
Height527.7 m (1,731 ft 4 in)
Top floor522 m (1,712 ft 7 in)[[1]](#cite_note-1)

[Kohn Pedersen Fox](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox) (Concept & Design);

China Zun

[Traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters)中國尊
Transcriptions
[Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese)
[Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin)Zhōngguó Zūn
[IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA/Mandarin)[[ʈʂʊ́ŋ.kwǒ tswə́n]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Mandarin)

CITIC Tower
[Simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters)中信集团总部大楼
[Traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters)中信集團總部大樓
Transcriptions
[Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese)
[Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin)Zhōngxìn Jítuán Zǒngbù Dàlóu

CITIC Tower, also known as China Zun,[[a]](#cite_note-3) is a [supertall](/wiki/Supertall) [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) in the [Central Business District](/wiki/Beijing_central_business_district) of [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing), China. The 109-story, 528-metre (1,732 ft) building constructed by China Construction Third Engineering Bureau is the [tallest in the city](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Beijing), surpassing the [China World Trade Center Tower III](/wiki/China_World_Trade_Center_Tower_III) by 190 metres (620 ft).[[3]](#cite_note-Xinhua-4) On 18 August 2016, CITIC Tower surpassed [China World Trade Center Tower III](/wiki/China_World_Trade_Center_Tower_III) in height, becoming Beijing's tallest building.[[4]](#cite_note-ECNS-5) The tower structurally topped out on 9 July 2017,[[5]](#cite_note-6) fully topped out on 18 August 2017, and was completed in late 2018, making CITIC Tower the tallest completed building of 2018.[[6]](#cite_note-7) As of 2024, it is the tallest building with a rooftop helipad in the world.[[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed)]

The nickname "China Zun" comes from the [zun](/wiki/Zun), an ancient Chinese wine vessel which inspired the building design, according to the developers, the [CITIC Group](/wiki/CITIC_Group). The groundbreaking ceremony of the building took place in Beijing on 19 September 2011, and the constructors expected to finish the project within five years. CITIC Tower is [Northern China](/wiki/North_China)'s third-tallest building after [Goldin Finance 117](/wiki/Goldin_Finance_117) and [Chow Tai Fook Binhai Center](/wiki/Tianjin_CTF_Finance_Centre) in [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin).

[Farrells](/wiki/Farrells) produced the tower's land bid concept design, with [Kohn Pedersen Fox](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox) assuming the project and completing a 14-month-long concept design process after the client had won the bid.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

China Zun is a mixed-use building, featuring 60 floors of office space, 20 floors of luxury apartments and 20 floors of hotel with 300 rooms.[[8]](#cite_note-9) There will be a rooftop garden on the top floor at 522 m (1,713 ft) high.

The tower is likely to remain the tallest building in Beijing for the foreseeable future, as in 2018 authorities capped new projects in the central business district to a height of no more than 180 m (590 ft) in a bid to reduce congestion.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

In April 2018, Hong Kong newspaper [Ming Pao](/wiki/Ming_Pao) reported that China Zun's top three floors, Levels 106–107 and an observatory on level 108, was to be expropriated by the national-security apparatus, since the entire [Zhongnanhai](/wiki/Zhongnanhai) complex, the headquarters of the [Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party) and the [State Council of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/State_Council_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China), could be seen from the top of the skyscraper with the naked eye on a clear day. It is also said[[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions)] that with high-end [telescopes](/wiki/Telescope) and other monitoring equipment, the day-to-day lives and activities of the Party and State Leaders could be seen.[[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement) – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:China_Zun#Dubious)] The building was ordered to be rectified for "fire safety issues" by the authorities, but the CITIC Group was unable to reveal the specific reason for rectification. It 
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="b3695f570dd5c3b8">>>"
}

Shanghai World Financial Center
上海环球金融中心

/wiki/File:%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E5%9B%BD%E9%99%85%E9%87%91%E8%9E%8D%E4%B8%AD%E5%BF%83.jpgThe Shanghai World Financial Center in March 2017

References
[[2]](#cite_note-skyscraperCenter-2)[[3]](#cite_note-skyscraperpage-3)[[4]](#cite_note-pbs-4)[[5]](#cite_note-chinadaily-5)

The Shanghai World Financial Center (SWFC; [Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters): [上海](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7)[环球](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%8E%AF%E7%90%83)[金融](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%87%91%E8%9E%8D)[中心](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%AD%E5%BF%83); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Shànghǎi Huánqiú Jīnróng Zhōngxīn, [Shanghainese](/wiki/Shanghainese): Zånhae Guejieu Cinyon Tsonsin) is a [supertall](/wiki/Supertall_building) [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) located in the [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong) district of [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai). It was designed by [Kohn Pedersen Fox](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox) and developed by the [Mori Building Company](/wiki/Mori_Building_Company), with Leslie E. Robertson Associates as its structural engineer and [China State Construction Engineering Corporation](/wiki/China_State_Construction_Engineering_Corporation) and Shanghai Construction (Group) General Co. as its main contractor. It is a mixed-use skyscraper, consisting of offices, hotels, conference rooms, and a ground-floor shopping mall.

On 14 September 2007, the skyscraper was [topped out](/wiki/Topped_out) at 492 meters (1,614.2 ft),[[1]](#cite_note-ssc010720-1)[[6]](#cite_note-6) making it the [2nd tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings)[[7]](#cite_note-7) on completion (the tallest at the time being [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101)), the tallest building in the world by roof height only, and [the tallest in mainland China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China).[[8]](#cite_note-8) The SWFC opened to the public on 28 August 2008, with its (since closed) [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) opening on 30 August. The observation deck offered views from 474 m (1,555 ft) above ground level.[[9]](#cite_note-bbc_open-9)

[Park Hyatt Shanghai](/wiki/Hyatt) is the tower's hotel component, comprising 174 rooms and suites occupying the 79th to the 93rd floors, which at the time of completion was the highest hotel in the world. It is now the third-highest hotel in the world after the [Ritz-Carlton, Hong Kong](/wiki/The_Ritz-Carlton,_Hong_Kong), which occupies floors 102 to 118 of the [International Commerce Centre](/wiki/International_Commerce_Centre).[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items)]

The SWFC has been lauded for its design, and in 2008 it was named by architects as the year's best-completed skyscraper.[[11]](#cite_note-reuters-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2013, the SWFC was exceeded in height by the adjacent [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower),[[13]](#cite_note-500Metres-13) which is China's tallest structure as of 2023. Together, The Shanghai World Financial Center, The [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower) and The [Jin Mao Tower](/wiki/Jin_Mao_Tower) form the world's first adjacent grouping of three supertall skyscrapers.

Designed by American architectural firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox), the 100-story tower was originally planned for construction in 1997, but work was temporarily interrupted by the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis), and was later paused to accommodate design changes by the Mori Building Company. The building of the tower was financed by several multinational firms, including Chinese, Japanese, and Hong Kong banks, as well as by the Japanese developer and American and European investors. The American investment bank [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morga

Shanghai World Financial Center
上海环球金融中心

/wiki/File:%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7%E5%9B%BD%E9%99%85%E9%87%91%E8%9E%8D%E4%B8%AD%E5%BF%83.jpgThe Shanghai World Financial Center in March 2017

[[2]](#cite_note-skyscraperCenter-2)[[3]](#cite_note-skyscraperpage-3)[[4]](#cite_note-pbs-4)[[5]](#cite_note-chinadaily-5)

The Shanghai World Financial Center (SWFC; [Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters): [上海](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%8A%E6%B5%B7)[环球](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%8E%AF%E7%90%83)[金融](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%87%91%E8%9E%8D)[中心](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%AD%E5%BF%83); [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin): Shànghǎi Huánqiú Jīnróng Zhōngxīn, [Shanghainese](/wiki/Shanghainese): Zånhae Guejieu Cinyon Tsonsin) is a [supertall](/wiki/Supertall_building) [skyscraper](/wiki/Skyscraper) located in the [Pudong](/wiki/Pudong) district of [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai). It was designed by [Kohn Pedersen Fox](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox) and developed by the [Mori Building Company](/wiki/Mori_Building_Company), with Leslie E. Robertson Associates as its structural engineer and [China State Construction Engineering Corporation](/wiki/China_State_Construction_Engineering_Corporation) and Shanghai Construction (Group) General Co. as its main contractor. It is a mixed-use skyscraper, consisting of offices, hotels, conference rooms, and a ground-floor shopping mall.

On 14 September 2007, the skyscraper was [topped out](/wiki/Topped_out) at 492 meters (1,614.2 ft),[[1]](#cite_note-ssc010720-1)[[6]](#cite_note-6) making it the [2nd tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings)[[7]](#cite_note-7) on completion (the tallest at the time being [Taipei 101](/wiki/Taipei_101)), the tallest building in the world by roof height only, and [the tallest in mainland China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China).[[8]](#cite_note-8) The SWFC opened to the public on 28 August 2008, with its (since closed) [observation deck](/wiki/Observation_deck) opening on 30 August. The observation deck offered views from 474 m (1,555 ft) above ground level.[[9]](#cite_note-bbc_open-9)

[Park Hyatt Shanghai](/wiki/Hyatt) is the tower's hotel component, comprising 174 rooms and suites occupying the 79th to the 93rd floors, which at the time of completion was the highest hotel in the world. It is now the third-highest hotel in the world after the [Ritz-Carlton, Hong Kong](/wiki/The_Ritz-Carlton,_Hong_Kong), which occupies floors 102 to 118 of the [International Commerce Centre](/wiki/International_Commerce_Centre).[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items)]

The SWFC has been lauded for its design, and in 2008 it was named by architects as the year's best-completed skyscraper.[[11]](#cite_note-reuters-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2013, the SWFC was exceeded in height by the adjacent [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower),[[13]](#cite_note-500Metres-13) which is China's tallest structure as of 2023. Together, The Shanghai World Financial Center, The [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower) and The [Jin Mao Tower](/wiki/Jin_Mao_Tower) form the world's first adjacent grouping of three supertall skyscrapers.

Designed by American architectural firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox), the 100-story tower was originally planned for construction in 1997, but work was temporarily interrupted by the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis), and was later paused to accommodate design changes by the Mori Building Company. The building of the tower was financed by several multinational firms, including Chinese, Japanese, and Hong Kong banks, as well as by the Japanese developer and American and European investors. The American investment bank [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morga
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="814a785d1bf2ead5">>>

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

International Commerce Centre
環球貿易廣場

/wiki/File:International_Commerce_Centre_(Hong_Kong)_logo.jpg
/wiki/File:International_Commerce_Centre_201008.jpg
Interactive map of the International Commerce Centre area

General information
StatusCompleted
Type[Commercial offices](/wiki/Commercial_office), [hotel](/wiki/Hotel)
Location1 [Austin Road West](/wiki/Austin_Road_West), [Kowloon](/wiki/Kowloon), Hong Kong
Construction started2002; 24 years ago
Completed2010; 16 years ago[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
ManagementHarbour Vantage[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Height
Architectural484 m (1,588 ft)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Tip484 m (1,588 ft)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Technical details
Floor count108 above ground[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Design and construction
Architect[Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox_Associates)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Developer[Sun Hung Kai Properties Limited](/wiki/Sun_Hung_Kai_Properties_Limited)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Structural engineer[Arup](/wiki/Arup_Group)[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)
Main contractorChina State Construction Engineering Corporation;[[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1) Sanfield Building Contractors Limited [[1]](#cite_note-SkyScraperCenter-1)

The International Commerce Centre ([Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters): 環球貿易廣場; [Jyutping](/wiki/Jyutping): waan4kau4 mau6jik6 gwong2coeng4) is a 108-storey, 484 m (1,588 ft) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers) in [West Kowloon](/wiki/West_Kowloon), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong), resting atop the [Elements](/wiki/Elements,_Hong_Kong) mall and near two [MTR](/wiki/MTR) Stations ([Kowloon](/wiki/Kowloon_station_(MTR)) and [Austin Station](/wiki/Austin_Station_(MTR))). It is [the world's 13th tallest building by height](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings), [10th tallest by number of floors](/wiki/List_of_buildings_with_100_floors_or_more), and [Hong Kong's tallest](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Hong_Kong), as well as the only building in the city with over 100 storeys. The official height is 484 m (1,588 ft), which includes the 6 m (20 ft) tall [parapets](/wiki/Parapet) on the roof.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It was the [fourth tallest building in Asia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Asia) and also the fourth tallest building in the world when completed in 2010.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

/wiki/File:Tallest_buildings_in_Asia.jpgInternational Commerce Centre compared with other tallest buildings in Asia.
The south side of the building faces [Victoria Harbour](/wiki/Victoria_Harbour).

The height had been scaled back from earlier plans due to regulations that did not allow buildings to be taller than the surrounding mountains. The original proposal for this building was called Kowloon Station Phase 7 and it was designed to be 574 m (1,883 ft) tall with 102 floors.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The tower was designed by the American architectural firm [Kohn Pedersen Fox Associates](/wiki/Kohn_Pedersen_Fox_Associates) (KPF) in association with [Wong & Ouyang](/wiki/Wong_%26_Ouyang). It was built by [China State Construction Engineering Corporation](/wiki/China_State_Construction_Engineering_Corporation) and Sanfield building constructor limited, a construction subsidiary of [Sun Hung Kai Properties Limited](/wiki/Sun_Hung_Kai_Properties_Limited).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Construction work was temporarily halted[[6]](#cite_note-6) on 13 September 2009, due to a lift shaft accident that killed six workers.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The top floor is numbered as "118". However, levels with "4" in the last digit were skipped because it sounds like "death" in [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese) and [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese) ([tetraphobia](/wiki/Tetraphobia)), similar to Western superstition about the number 13 ([triskaidekaphobia](/wiki/Triskaidekaphobia)). As a result, the remaining storeys' floor numbers are also increased; e.g. the top floor of the 68-storey building, [The Cullinan](/wiki/The_Cullinan), is numbered 93.

Except for level 3, 103 and 113, floor numbers with "3" in the last digit were also skipped, floor numbers 5, 6, 7, 26, 28, 29 and 105 were skipped as well for unknown reasons. They are currently replaced by floor numbers with "M" & "R", which stand for "Mechanical" and "Refuge". Although the floor numbers are missing, they still show on the elevator's screen of Sky100 and Skydining 101 while going up and down.

28 floor numbers were skipped: 4, 5, 6, 7, 13, 14, 23, 24, 26, 28, 29, 33, 34, 43, 44, 53, 54, 63, 64, 73, 74, 83, 84, 93, 94, 104, 105, 114

18 floor numbers were added: UG, M1-1, M1-2, M1-3, M1-5, R1, R2, M2-1, M2-2, R3, M3-1, M3-2, R4, M4-1, M4-2, M4-3, M5, M6

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Wuhan Greenland Center
武汉绿地中心

/wiki/File:(CHN-Hubei)_Wuhan_Greenland_Center_2024-12-01.jpg
Interactive map of the Wuhan Greenland Center area

Alternative namesGreenland Center, WGC

Other information
Parking1051
References
[[1]](#cite_note-skyscraper-center-1)[[2]](#cite_note-ASGG1-2)

Wuhan Greenland Center[[3]](#cite_note-3) is a 476-metre (1,562 ft) [supertall skyscraper](/wiki/List_of_supertall_skyscrapers) in [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan), China. It is the [ninth-tallest building in China](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_China) and the [fourteenth-tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings).[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) The tower was originally planned to be 636 metres (2,087 ft), but was redesigned mid-construction due to [airspace](/wiki/Airspace) regulations so its height does not exceed 502 metres (1,650 ft) above ground level.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The building was designed by [Adrian Smith + Gordon Gill Architecture](/wiki/Adrian_Smith_%2B_Gordon_Gill_Architecture) in conjunction with [Thornton Tomasetti](/wiki/Thornton_Tomasetti) Engineers won the design competition[[6]](#cite_note-ASGG2-6) to build the tower for [Greenland Group](/wiki/Greenland_Group), a real estate developer owned by the Shanghai city government. Construction started in 2012 and had been put on-hold numerous times following the redesign in mid-2017 ranging from financial problems, to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_mainland_China). The building finally [topped-out](/wiki/Topping_out) in late 2020 and was completed in 2022. The Wuhan Greenland Center is Central China's tallest building with a cost of US$4.5 billion, mostly due to the number of times it had been put on-hold.

The original plan for the building was to have it rise 636 metres (2,087 ft),[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) surpassing the [Shanghai Tower](/wiki/Shanghai_Tower) by only 4.3 metres (14 ft) and the [Tokyo Skytree](/wiki/Tokyo_Skytree) by 2.1 metres (7 ft), making it the second tallest man-made structure in the world. The tower was also supposed to have 126 floors, the second most of any building in the world, as well. When the Wuhan Greenland Center reached its 96th floor, construction was halted due to airspace restrictions which led to its subsequent redesign to a 476 metres (1,562 ft)[[7]](#cite_note-7) building instead of a 636 metres (2,087 ft) building. The Wuhan Greenland Center is currently the [14th tallest building in the world](/wiki/List_of_future_tallest_buildings).
